coredata.dc:title: Application of cerium–nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots to the detection of tetracyclines residues and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ziting
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: We synthesized cerium–nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Ce–N–CDs) with citric acid, ethylenediamine, and cerium nitrate via the hydrothermal method. Carbon quantum dots synthesized by changing the addition amount of ethylenediamine and cerium nitrate, heating temperature, and time exhibited good water solubility and high quantum yield (QY = 93.14%). The carbon quantum dots were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and other characterization techniques. Results confirmed successful Ce–N–CDs formation. The Ce–N–CDs showed high detection selectivity to tetracyclines (TCs) residues. When the experimental conditions were optimized and the tetracyclines member doxycycline (DC) was selected as the representative, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the Ce–N–CDs, exhibited a good linear relationship with DC concentration within 0.4–35 µM with a detection limit of 0.25 µM. The method was successfully applied to the determination of DC and total TCs in milk and pork. Therefore, this fluorescent probe can be used as a sensitive platform for TCs analysis. The Ce–N–CDs synthesized via the hydrothermal method had low cytotoxicity and did not affect cell viability. The possible application of the Ce–N–CDs in cell imaging was explored.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ce–N–CDs
Tetracyclines
Doxycycline
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N–CDs, Ce–CDs and Ce–N–CDs
Detection of DC and total TCs in milk and pork samples
Quenching effect of DC on the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs
Selectivity and interferences of TCs detection
The total TCs recognised by Ce–N–CDs fluorescence probe
Cytotoxicity and bioimaging of the Ce–N–CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM images and size distribution histogram of Ce-N-CDs; b) HRTEM images of Ce-N-CDs; c) AFM images, and d) XRD patterns of the Ce–N–CDs.

a) XPS spectrum of the Ce–N–CDs; b) UV–vis spectra of Ce–N–CDs (black) and DC (red) and excitation and emission spectra (blue) of Ce–N–CDs; c) Infrared spectrum of the Ce–N–CDs、N–CDs and Ce–CDs; d) Zeta potential of the Ce–N–CDs.
FL spectra of Ce–N–CDs after adding different concentrations of DC.
Comparison of the sensing performance of different methods for TCs detection.
Recovery and analysis of DC, DC + TC, and CTC + DC in actual food samples.

head.ce:title: Application of cerium–nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots to the detection of tetracyclines residues and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We synthesized cerium–nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Ce–N–CDs) with citric acid, ethylenediamine, and cerium nitrate via the hydrothermal method. Carbon quantum dots synthesized by changing the addition amount of ethylenediamine and cerium nitrate, heating temperature, and time exhibited good water solubility and high quantum yield (QY = 93.14%). The carbon quantum dots were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and other characterization techniques. Results confirmed successful Ce–N–CDs formation. The Ce–N–CDs showed high detection selectivity to tetracyclines (TCs) residues. When the experimental conditions were optimized and the tetracyclines member doxycycline (DC) was selected as the representative, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the Ce–N–CDs, exhibited a good linear relationship with DC concentration within 0.4–35 µM with a detection limit of 0.25 µM. The method was successfully applied to the determination of DC and total TCs in milk and pork. Therefore, this fluorescent probe can be used as a sensitive platform for TCs analysis. The Ce–N–CDs synthesized via the hydrothermal method had low cytotoxicity and did not affect cell viability. The possible application of the Ce–N–CDs in cell imaging was explored.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel fluorescent nanomaterial first discovered in 2004 [1], carbon quantum dots (CDs) have excellent optical properties, good water solubility, simple and rapid preparation, easy functionalization, and very low toxicity [2–3]. After years of development in food analysis [4], bioimaging [5–7], and biosensing [8–9], it has broad application prospects. Previous studies developed various methods and utilized different raw materials to improve the quantum yield (QY) [10] of CDs. CDs usually have a strong absorption within the ultraviolet range, mainly within the blue light range. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QD) and CDs are different. The optical properties (i.e., absorption, excitation, and emission) of the latter are highly related to the nature of reaction material, reaction temperature, time, and other factors; therefore, its PL properties can be adjusted [11]. Doping and modifying heteroatoms and surface passivation can enhance CDs performance. Some studies confirmed that doping with CDs heteroatoms can effectively regulate band gap and electron density, improve chemical activity, and enhance CDs performance in various applications [12–14]. Markovic et al. Synthesized F and Cl Co doped CDs, which have good antioxidant activity and have been successfully applied in biological imaging. [15].Doping of metal ions may not only allow the adjustment of the properties of CDs and metal ions but also increase quantum yield. Liu et al. synthesized terbium-modified CDs for dipicolinic acid (DPA) detection [16]. Mondal et al. Synthesized Eu and Tb Co doped CDs for Cr (VI) detection [17]. As far as we know, Ce–N–CDs have not been developed yet. In this study, Ce–N–CDs with good PL properties and high QY were prepared via the one-step hydrothermal method. This probe has great potential for detecting tetracyclines (TCs) residues in food.

Antibiotics are vital for human and animal health as they are used to treat and/or prevent diseases. TCs-antibiotic drugs include doxycycline (DC), oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), and chlortetracycline (CTC). These antibiotics have broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and can resist various bacterial infections [18–19]. Moreover, these type of antibiotics are extensively utilized in medicine and food additives [20]. Bacterial growth is inhibited under the action of TCS drugs, which interfere with protein synthesis or destroy membrane after diffusion and infiltration of bacterial cells in a passive way. For over 75 years, TCs drugs have been used in in the prevention and treatment of various human and animal diseases [21]. TCs drugs are widely used in controlling animal husbandry microbial diseases because they have high bioavailability and inexpensive. These features make TCs drugs as production promoters [22–23]. However, numerous bacterial species and strains have developed tolerance to TCs because of their widespread application [24]. Moreover, excessive use of TCs in animal husbandry and breeding leave TCs residues in beef, shrimp, fish, honey, and other foods [25–28]. TCs residues pose a serious threat to human health because they can cause serious side effects, such as liver damage, allergies, and yellowing of teeth [19,29–31]. To ensure food safety, China, the European Union, and other countries have legally set maximum residue standards for various antibiotic drugs [32]. At present, the residue limit of tetracycline for aquatic products is 100 μg/kg in China's national standard, 200 μg/kg for fish and shellfish muscle by Codex Alimentarius Commission of the World Health Organization, and 10 μg/kg of oxytetracycline in imported aquatic products by the United States.Therefore, rapid, economical, and practical methods for TCs residue detection in food products must be developed. Various detection techniques, such as enzyme-linked immunoadsorption test [33], high-performance liquid chromatography [28,34–35], and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [36], have already been applied or developed for TCs residue detection. Although these methods are accurate, the equipment is expensive, the operation is complex, the sample pretreatment is complex, time-consuming and high cost, which limits their further use. Thus, a rapid method for TCs residue detection should be designed. The synthesis of carbon quantum dots is simple, environmentally friendly, low cost, fast response and high accuracy. It has been proved that using fluorescence signal to detect the target is an effective method [37–40].

A novel quantum dot, namely, Ce–N–CDs, with high fluorescence properties was developed herein. The one-step hydrothermal method is used to synthesize the Ce–N–CDs. The materials needed for this process are citric acid, ethylenediamine and cerium nitrate. The conditions used for the synthesis were further optimized by adjusting the addition amount, reaction time, and temperature of ethylenediamine and cerium nitrate to improve the fluorescence intensity and QY of the Ce–N–CDs. Moreover, the Ce–N–CDs were highly sensitive and selective to TCs residues in food samples (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N–CDs, Ce–CDs and Ce–N–CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Materials with citric acid as carbon source and ethylenediamine (nitrogen source) and cerium nitrate as dopants were applied to synthesize the N–CDs, Ce–CDs and Ce–N–CDs with the method of hydrothermal. At the same time, the amount of ethylenediamine and cerium nitrate, the hydrothermal time and temperature were optimized.

The amount of material incorporation, reaction time, and temperature were optimized. The Ce–N–CDs with the highest QY and the best water solubility were selected for TCs residue detection in various food products after (Fig. S1).

First, 0.10 g/L of the standard Ce–N–CDs solution required for subsequent experiments was prepared to use ultra-pure water and then stored. The TCs member DC was used as the representative; In a 10 mL colorimetric tube, 20 μL of the Ce–N–CDs solution, 250 μL of phosphate buffer (PBS) (0.2 M, pH = 4.0) and various concentrations of doxycycline solutions were added in turn. The last step is to adjust the volume of the solution to 5 mL with ultra-pure water. The solution at room temperature presents uniform reaction. Fluorescence spectra of Ce–N–CDs excited at 340 nm were collected. The slit widths of excitation and emission are 5 nm and 20 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DC and total TCs in milk and pork samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the probe in detecting DC and total TCs from actual samples was investigated using milk and pork samples were purchased from a local market (Linfen, Shanxi). The samples were processed following previously described methods [41–42]. Milk and trichloroacetic acid were pretreated via the procedure in Ref. [42]to remove proteins. The milk sample and 1% (v/v) trichloroacetic acid solution were added to the centrifuge tube, then ultrasonicated for 20 min. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and then filtered through a 0.2 µm membrane to eliminate particles. NaOH was used to adjust pH to 7, and the solution was stored in a refrigerator before use. Subsequently, 2.0 g of the pork sample was mashed and then immersed in 5 mL of acetonitrile. The obtained samples were homogenized by eddy current for 3 min, sonicated for 15 min, and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. Finally, the supernatant was diluted with 250 mL water and stored in a refrigerator before use. DC, DC + TC, and CTC + DC of various concentrations were added to the milk and pork samples, and their concentrations were determined to detect total TCs.

The toxicity of the Ce–N–CDs to HepG2 cells can be determined by using 33-(4,5-Dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazole bromide) (MTT). Normal HepG2 cells were added to a 96-well plate and incubated in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 ℃ for 24 h. Different concentrations of the Ce-N-CDs (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 μL) were then added and dispersed in the cell culture medium, and culturing continued for 6 h and then rinsed with PBS (pH 7.4). Untreated normal cells were taken as the control. The absorbance of the hole was measured using a microplate reader. Cell proliferation rate was calculated using the following formula (1):
(1)
VR = A /
A 0
× 100 %
where A is the absorbance of the probe-treated cell, and A0 is the absorbance of the treatment.

After the cells were cultured, 10 μL Ce–N–CDs solution was added into the medium containing cells, incubated at 37 ℃ for 2 h, washed with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) for 3 times, and fluorescence imaging was performed under laser confocal microscope. After that, 10 μM DC solution was added to the above culture concentration and incubated for 2 h for fluorescence imaging.

Ce–N–CDs with high QY and optimal fluorescence intensity under constant citric acid condition were obtained by first investigating the effects of doping amount of ethylenediamine and cerium nitrate on fluorescence intensity. At 2 h and 150 ℃, five different samples were prepared, which were individually labelled as Ce–N–CDs1, Ce–N–CDs2, Ce–N–CDs3, Ce–N–CDs4, and Ce–N–CDs5. When 1 mL ethylenediamine was mixed with 0.5 g (Ce–N–CDs1), 1 g (Ce–N–CDs2), and 1.5 g (Ce–N–CDs3) cerium nitrate, the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs increased as cerium nitrate concentration increased. The maximum fluorescence intensity was reached when 1 g cerium nitrate was added. However, the fluorescence intensity of the cerium–nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots will decrease when 1.5 g cerium nitrate was added. When 2 (Ce–N–CDs4) and 3 mL (Ce–N–CDs5) ethylenediamine were added while cerium nitrate concentration was maintained at 1 g, the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs continued to decrease (Fig. S1a). This may be caused by excessive incorporation of cerium nitrate and ethylenediamine, and the increase of solution concentration will make Ce–N–CDs agglomerate, resulting in the gradual decrease of fluorescence intensity [43]. When 1 mL ethylenediamine and 1 g cerium nitrate were added, the maximum fluorescence intensity was achieved and QY reached 73.60% (Table S2).

Results showed that preparation time (2, 4, and 8 h) had a substantial effect on the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs over time (Fig. S1b). Continuous heating for a long time will lead to excessive carbonization of the precursor and the formation of other by-products, which will affect the fluorescence intensity of Ce–N–CDs [44]. Therefore, 2 h is the best preparation time.

With the continuous change of reaction temperature during preparation, when the nucleation temperature is met, the precursor starts to form carbon nucleus after continuous crosslinking polymerization, which indicates that the carbon quantum dots have been formed. At 2 h of reaction time, 2 g citric acid, 1 mL ethylenediamine, and 1 g cerium nitrate were mixed, and three samples were prepared at180 ℃, 200 ℃, and 240 ℃. The fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs increased as temperature increase, and the maximum fluorescence intensity was achieved at 200 ℃ (Fig. S1c). The fluorescence intensity began to decrease at 240 ℃. The reason for this phenomenon may be that when the temperature is lower than the nucleation temperature (180 ℃), the precursor of carbon quantum dots with fluorescent groups will be formed. When the temperature rises to 180 ℃, the carbon core gradually forms, and the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots is gradually enhanced. When the temperature rises to 200 ℃, the obtained carbon quantum dots have small carbon cores and a large number of fluorescent groups are attached on the surface, At this time, the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots reaches the strongest; the pyrolysis temperature increases to 240 ℃, the fluorescent groups formed at low temperature gradually transform into carbon nuclei during high temperature pyrolysis. With the temperature increasing, the size of carbon core of carbon quantum dots increases gradually, the surface fluorescent groups gradually decrease, and the fluorescence intensity also decreases [45]. Hence, the optimal preparation conditions of 2 g citric acid, 1 mL ethylenediamine, and 1 g cerium nitrate at 200 ℃ for 2 h were implemented for subsequent experiments.

According to Fig. 2 a, Ce–N–CDs prepared by hydrothermal method with citric acid, ethylenediamine and cerium nitrate under the optimal conditions are uniformly distributed like a sphere with an average particle size of 4.16 nm and no obvious aggregation. The Ce–N–CDs were well dispersed, uniform in size, and with evident lattice fringes (Fig. 2b). The height of the Ce–N–CDs as determined via AFM was about 1.5 nm (Fig. 2c). The crystalline structure of the Ce–N–CDs was analyzed via XRD. A wide peak (Fig. 2d) corresponding to the lattice spacing of the carbon-based material (002) was observed at 23° [46]. The Ce–N–CDs emitted blue light under ultraviolet light, indicating that the Ce–N–CDs had PL characteristics. According to Fig S2a-c, when CDs, N–CDs and Ce–N–CDs are excited at 340 nm, the maximum emission peaks of CDs, N–CDs and Ce–N–CDs are all concentrated at 440 nm. According to Fig S2d, the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots is greatly enhanced due to the doping of N, when Ce and N are Co doped, the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots is further enhanced.

The chemical state and surface composition of Ce–N–CDs were analyzed by XPS. In Fig. 3 a, C1s (285 ev), O1s (532 ev), N1s (401 ev) and Ce 3d (904 ev) prove the existence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and cerium, which are the same as the results of element analysis. Therefore, it can be seen that cerium exists in the form of Ce3+ and Ce4+ (Fig. S3). The UV absorption spectra of the Ce–N–CDs are shown in Fig. 3b (black curve). The major absorption peak was at 348 nm, which could have been caused by n–π* electron transition [47–48], whereas the weak shoulder peak at 242 nm could have been caused by π–π* transitions in the carbonyl and amide bonds (CC/CO). According to FTIR spectra (Fig. 3C), due to the co doping of Ce and N, the surface of Ce–N–CDs rich in oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups, which is more abundant than that of N–CDs and Ce–CDs, thus increasing the water solubility and stability of. The peak values of 3415 cm−1 and 3423 cm−1 were attributed to the OH/NH stretching vibration peaks of the carboxyl and amide groups of the Ce–N–CDs, respectively. The CH stretching vibration peak appeared at 2929 cm−1. The two characteristic peaks of 1558 cm−1 and 1654 cm−1 were attributed to CO stretching vibrations. The tensile vibration peaks of CN (1384 cm−1) and COC (1224 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1) were also obtained(Fig. 3c), therefore, the surface of Ce–N–CDs contains hydroxyl, unsaturated CH, carbonyl, olefin, carboxyl and other groups. The zeta potential value of the Ce–N–CDs in water was −2.14 mv (Fig. 3d).

The QY of quinine sulfate in 0.10 m H2SO4 solution (QY = 0.56) was taken as the FL standard to investigate further Ce–N–CDs QY under various reaction conditions. The fluorescence QY of the Ce–N–CDs was calculated using the following formula (2):
(2)
ɸ
x
=
ɸ
st
(
I x
/
I
st
)
(
Ƞ
x
2
/
Ƞ
st
2
)
(
A
st
/
A x
)
where
ɸ
x
is QY, I is the integral area of fluorescence emission intensity measured at 340 nm excitation and 440 nm emission wavelength, Ƞ is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the UV absorption intensity of the Ce–N–CDs at 340 nm. Moreover, st represents the standard sample, whereas x represents the test sample., The highest QY (93.14%) of the Ce–N–CDs synthesized via the one-step hydrothermal method was reached under the best preparation conditions (Table S2). Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of Ce–N–CDs was about 16.98 ns (Fig. S4).

Exploration of the experimental conditions enhanced the detection performance of the sensor. The effects of pH and reaction time on the detection performance of the sensor were evaluated using the sensor platform designed herein. First, the effects of pH on the fluorescence quenching of the sensing system were optimized. At 0.2 M PBS concentration, the effects of DC on the fluorescence quenching of the Ce–N–CDs were within the pH range of 4.0–9.0 (Fig. S5a). Quenching efficiency was calculated as follows:
(3)
Effq
( % )
= （
F
0
- F ） /
F
0
where F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs at 440 nm in the absence of DC, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs at 440 nm in the presence of DC. The strongest fluorescence quenching efficiency was achieved at pH 4.0, and quenching efficiency progressively decreased within the pH range of 5.0–9.0 (Fig. S5a). Thus, pH 4.0 was chosen as the best pH value for the fluorescence quenching system.

In addition, we studied the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence quenching efficiency at pH = 4.0. As shown in Fig. S5b, we recorded the effects of 1 min, 3 min, 5 min, 10 min, 15 min, 20 min, 30 min and 40 min on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Ce–N–CDs/DC system, respectively. It can be seen that the reaction time has no effect on the fluorescence quenching efficiency. Therefore, 5 min was chosen as the best reaction time for fluorescence quenching system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of DC on the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 reveals that when the pH of PBS was 4.0, the FL intensity of Ce–N–CDs declined gradually with the addition of different concentrations of DC solution to Ce–N–CDs (20 µL, 0.10 g L−1) solution. When DC solution was added to 35 μM, the fluorescence intensity of quantum dots decreased by 62%, and the fluorescence spectrum was collected at 340 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 4). DC concentration within the range of 0.4–35 μM showed a good linear relationship between Ce–N–CDs fluorescence quenching efficiency and DC (Fig. S6). The linear equation was F/F0 = −0.0133x + 0.8222 (R2 = 0.99021), where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs in the absence and presence of DC, respectively. The detection limit (3σ/κ) of the constructed system was 0.25 µM. Various methods for detecting TCs residues have been developed in recent years (Table 1 ). Compared with these methods, the method developed in this study is uncomplicated, rapid, environmentally friendly, obtains high QY, has a wide linear range, and inexpensive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interferences of TCs detection
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. S7a, the similarity of chemical composition and structure of TC, DC, OTC and CTC leads to basically the same change of fluorescence spectrum of quantum dots. Therefore, DC is taken as an example to confirm the interference and selectivity of cerium and nitrogen Co doped carbon quantum dots on TCS detection. We chose 30 μM doxycycline and 10 mM other antibiotics, metal ions, anions, amino acids of to Ce–N–CDs Interference of fluorescence intensity. The black bar in Fig. S7b shows the quenching efficiency of the Ce–N–CDs. Unlike DC, the other antibiotics, metal ions, anions, and amino acids had negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity of the Ce–N–CDs, demonstrating that the Ce–N–CDs have very good selectivity for TCs residue detection. At the same time, the interference of other antibiotics, metal ions, anions, amino acids, etc. on the DC detection is evaluated. In Fig. S7b, the red bar shows that the fluorescence intensity of Ce–N–CDs /DC system does not change when other antibiotics, metal ions, anions and amino acids coexist with DC. It can be seen that the quantum dots have a very good anti-interference ability for DC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The total TCs recognised by Ce–N–CDs fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of total TCs in food was identified using the Ce–N–CDs. The fluorescence signals of different TCs were assessed if they were really additive. The samples were analyzed following a previously described method. The fluorescence intensities of mixtures of 30 µM of single TCs (TC, DC, OTC, and CTC), 30 µM of binary TCs (TC + DC), ternary TCs (TC + DC + OTC), and quaternary TCs (TC + DC + OTC + CTC) were measured. The fluorescence intensity of single TCs samples at the same concentration was consistent with that of TCs obtained at different mixtures (Fig. S7a). These results demonstrated that the Ce–N–CDs have good application prospects for detecting total TCs in food products.

As shown in Fig. 3b, DC has a wide absorption peak at 278 nm and 350 nm, while Ce–N–CDs has a wide absorption peak at 348 nm, which matches the excitation peak (~348 nm) of Ce–N–CDs, which produces an emission peak at 440 nm. Therefore, the strong overlap of doxycycline and Ce–N–CDs absorption spectra may lead to obvious internal filtering effect (IFE) [49–50], which leads to a significant decrease of fluorescence signal of Ce–N–CDs at 440 nm. At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime of Ce–N–CDs was calculated by fitting the fluorescence lifetime of Ce–N–CDs in the absence and presence of DC. According to Fig. S4, the fluorescence lifetime of Ce–N–CDs did not change. Therefore, according to the above results, the fluorescence quenching of Ce–N–CDs induced by DC may be attributed to the internal filtering effect (IFE).

As an antibiotic widely used in animal breeding, TCs are used to treat and prevent infection. Hence, detection of TCs residues in meat and milk products is important to ensure food safety. The feasibility of the analytical method described herein was evaluated by detecting TCs residues in actual samples via the standard addition method. No TCs were detected in the milk and pork samples (Table 2 ). The recovery rate of milk and pork samples mixed with different amounts of DC, DC + TC, and CTC + DC was calculated. The recovery rate of standard samples ranged from 92% to 107.06%, with a reproducibility of 1.05%–2.84% (RSD%, n = 3). These results validated that the Ce–N–CDs have very good accuracy, no obvious interference matrix, and have good application prospects in food monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and bioimaging of the Ce–N–CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The potential application of the Ce–N–CDs in bioimaging was explored. The cytotoxicity of the Ce–N–CDs was examined via the MTT method. At different Ce–N–CDs concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 μL), the value-added rate of cultured HepG2 cells was observed. Furthermore, the Ce–N–CDs did not exhibit obvious toxicity; even at 60 μL of Ce–N–CDs concentration, the cell proliferation rate remained at about 89% (Fig. S8). In practice, bioimaging requires very low concentrations (about 10 μL). Thus, the Ce–N–CDs can be potentially used in biological imaging.

Owing to the excellent biocompatibility, luminescence stability, and very low biotoxicity of the Ce–N–CDs, they were used in bioimaging experiments of HepG2 cells. The HepG2 cells displayed blue fluorescence at 405 nm photoexcitation (Fig. S9), indicating that the Ce–N–CDs were effectively internalized by the HepG2 cells. When the HepG2 cells were exposed to 10 µM of DC, the fluorescence of the Ce–N–CDs was evidently quenched, indicating that DC can be clearly detected in HepG2 cells. These results supported the usefulness of the Ce–N–CDs in biological imaging.

Ce–N–CDs with high QY, good water solubility, good fluorescence properties, good stability, and environmental protection were synthesized by optimizing the incorporation of citric acid, ethylenediamine, and cerium nitrate. The UV absorption peak of DC and the excitation peak of the Ce–N–CDs overlapped, resulting in IFE. Consequently, the fluorescence of the Ce–N–CDs was gradually quenched. Therefore, the analytical method developed herein can successfully detect DC and total TCs in actual food samples. Moreover, this novel method can be utilized in cell imaging of HepG2 cells and detecting intracellular TCs.

Ziting Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Zhefeng Fan: Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106139.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of tetracycline by microdroplet hydrodynamic adsorptive voltammetry using a multiwalled carbon nanotube paste rotating disk electrode
Anal. Letters.
